expectations.text: Presents an initial recommendation table before any changes
expectations.evidence: Turn 1 presents a full 8-row table with columns for #, Category, Recommendation, Rationale, and Status, covering Frontend, Backend, Database, Auth, Payments, AI/LLM, Deployment, and Extras before any user modifications.

expectations.text: When frontend changes from Next.js to Vite+React, explicitly identifies that backend and/or deployment are affected
expectations.evidence: Turn 3 explicitly states: 'Changing frontend from Next.js to Vite + React invalidates two downstream decisions: Backend (#2) ... Vite + React is a client-only SPA with no server-side API routes, so you now need a standalone backend. Deployment (#7) ... That advantage no longer applies with a Vite SPA.'

expectations.text: Resets affected downstream decisions in the table (back to pending/suggested status)
expectations.evidence: In the Turn 3 updated table, Backend (#2) and Deployment (#7) are both reset to '?' with status marker changed to the pending symbol (diamond), clearly showing they need re-evaluation.

expectations.text: Walks through invalidated stages to re-decide them with new context (e.g., backend now needed since Vite has no API routes)
expectations.evidence: Turns 3-5 walk through each invalidated stage sequentially. Turn 3 presents backend options (Express+tRPC, Fastify, Hono) noting Vite has no API routes. After the user picks Express+tRPC in Turn 4, Turn 4 presents deployment options (Railway, Fly.io, Render) appropriate for a Node server + SPA architecture. Turn 5 finalizes with Railway.

expectations.text: Does NOT invalidate unrelated decisions (e.g., database, auth should stay unchanged)
expectations.evidence: Turn 3 explicitly states: 'Auth (#4 Clerk) and Database (#3 Postgres + Drizzle) are not affected — Clerk works with any React frontend, and the database choice is independent of the frontend framework.' Both remain with checkmark status throughout all subsequent tables.

expectations.text: Final table is internally consistent — no decisions that assume Next.js remain after switching to Vite
expectations.evidence: The final table in Turn 5 shows: Frontend = Vite + React, Backend = Express + tRPC (standalone server, not Next.js API routes), Database = Postgres + Drizzle (unchanged, no Next.js dependency), Auth = Clerk (framework-agnostic), Deployment = Railway (suited for Node servers, replacing the Next.js-specific Vercel). No remnants of Next.js assumptions remain.
